coredata.dc:title: Core-shell structure DA-CDs/AuNPs for the recognition of fenamidone by surface-enhanced Raman scattering
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Kexiang
Li, Hong
Zhang, Qian
Yang, Dezhi
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile synthesis method for dopamine carbon dots-based Au nanoparticles (DA-CDs/AuNPs) by seed gold method was reported as the surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) booster. DA-CDs with rich in surface functional groups was synthesized using dopamine, citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors by a facile hydrothermal method, and can be used as the capping agents and reducing agents for the synthesis of DA-CDs/AuNPs. Due to the electromagnetic “hot spots” effect, DA-CDs/AuNPs with core–shell structure exhibited strong SERS activity. Based on the specific interaction of DA-CDs/AuNPs and fenamidone, a detection method of fenamidone was established with a low detection limit of 0.05 μg/mL. Finally, the SERS sensor was successfully applied to the detection of fenamidone in fruit with recoveries between 90.6 % and 98.7 %. The method here proposed can be reliably applied for fenamidone detection on fruits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface-enhanced Raman scattering
Dopamine carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Fenamidone
Fruits

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Computational procedure and recognition mechanism of fenamidone
Optimization of the fenamidone analytical system
Working curve for SERS detection of fenamidone
Selectivity and specificity of the DA-CDs/AuNPs-based SERS sensor
Detection of fenamidone on various fruit peels

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117) and the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2021T20200097).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of DA-CDs/AuNPs (a-c); SERS-enhancing effect of DA-CDs/AuNPs in the presence or absence of growth(d); UV–vis spectra of DA-CDs/AuNPs (e); Fluorescence spectra of DA-CDs/AuNPs (f); XPS spectra of nanozymes (g-k); Zeta potential image of DA-CDs/AuNPs in the presence or absence of fenamidone (l).

SERS spectra of different concentrations of fenamidone and DA-CDs/AuNPs system (a); Stability of DA-CDs/AuNPs substrates between sample batches (b-c); DFT calculated SERS and SERS spectra of fenamidone (d).
Different conditions effect on SERS intensities. DA-CDs/AuNPs concentration (a-b); The reaction time of DA-CDs/AuNPs(c-d).

SERS spectra for DA-CDs/AuNPs + fenamidone system with the different concentration of fenamidone from 0.08 ∼ 2.85 mg/mL (a); Linear curve of characteristic peak intensity at 1186 cm−1 and fenamidone concentration (b).
Selectivity of DA-CDs/AuNPs system for fenamidone detection. Interference of structural analogues (a); Interference of coexisting substances (b); Effect of metal ions (c and d).
Comparison of SERS spectra of standards and samples (a-d).
Schematic representation of the synthesis of DA-CDs/AuNPs.
The experimental and theoretical SERS bands.

row.entry: C–C swing on the benzene ring
C–C swing and stretching of the imidazole ring

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the analytical performance of sensor.
Determination of fenamidone in the fruit samples.

head.ce:title: Core-shell structure DA-CDs/AuNPs for the recognition of fenamidone by surface-enhanced Raman scattering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facile synthesis method for dopamine carbon dots-based Au nanoparticles (DA-CDs/AuNPs) by seed gold method was reported as the surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) booster. DA-CDs with rich in surface functional groups was synthesized using dopamine, citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors by a facile hydrothermal method, and can be used as the capping agents and reducing agents for the synthesis of DA-CDs/AuNPs. Due to the electromagnetic “hot spots” effect, DA-CDs/AuNPs with core–shell structure exhibited strong SERS activity. Based on the specific interaction of DA-CDs/AuNPs and fenamidone, a detection method of fenamidone was established with a low detection limit of 0.05 μg/mL. Finally, the SERS sensor was successfully applied to the detection of fenamidone in fruit with recoveries between 90.6 % and 98.7 %. The method here proposed can be reliably applied for fenamidone detection on fruits.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fenamidone is a new imidazoline broad-spectrum fungicide with strong toxicity, easy penetration and internal absorption, which is widely used in fungal diseases management of rice, fruit and vegetable crops [1]. Studies have shown that fenamidone has certain cytotoxicity and that its intensive use can produce residues on the surface of agricultural products. It is a potential risk for public health and environmental pollution. The negative effects of fenamidone to human health have increased the restrictions about its use in several countries. For example, the National Standard of China (GB 23200.8, GB23200.113) limited the presence of fenamidone in grapes to 0.6 mg/kg. Traditional methods for the determination of fenamidone on fruits, vegetables and other samples are usually based on gas chromatographic ion trap mass spectrometry (GC-ITMS) [2], ultra-high-performance liquid chromatographic tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) [3–5], gas chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [6,7], and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [8]. Although these methods are sufficiently accurate, they suffer from many drawbacks, such as requiring complicated sample pretreatment, time consuming, and high detection costs. There is a need to develop convenient and credible methods to monitor fenamidone residues in fruit to ensure food safety and quality.

SERS is an emerging technology with high sensitivity, rapid and exclusive molecular vibrational information of the analytes [9,10]. SERS combines nanotechnology and Raman spectroscopy, with simple preprocessing procedure, Raman signal amplification and provide rich molecular fingerprinting of analytes. It achieves robust, rapid and non-destructive identification of target molecules at the trace level. SERS has been utilized potentially for wide food safety applications, and shows great promise as a rapid and sensitive analytical method. The currently accepted mechanisms for SERS enhancement by researchers are the electromagnetic enhancement mechanism (EM) and the chemical enhancement mechanism (CM) [11]. EM is a physical enhancement model, caused by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) resulting from the excitation of incident light onto the surface of a nanostructure. CM indicates the charge transfer effect between the molecule and the metal substrate surface [12]. In SERS detection, the preparation of highly active SERS substrates is essential to achieve Raman signal enhancement of the analytes. The “hot spot” is a region of ultra-high electromagnetic field strength generated by the LSPR. Many efforts have been focused on synthesizing SERS substrates with more “hot spot” to enhance the enhancement factors, thus increase detection sensitivity. However, the biggest challenge for the practical application of SERS probes is the lack of efficient SERS substrates with homogeneous and dense “hot spot”.

The susceptibility of SERS substrate is determined by the degree of LSPR [13], which depends on the coupling between electromagnetic wave and metallic nanostructures. The LSPR can be simply tuned by changing the sizes, shapes or aggregates of metallic nanoparticles, for example, triangular, star-shaped, cubic or flower-like nanoparticles. Some studies have shown that the addition of other nanomaterials during the synthesis of SERS substrates can effectively adjust the morphology of the nanomaterials and contribute to the generation of more pronounced SERS signals.

Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most beneficial nanomaterials known to humanity [14]. CDs have been paid much attention for outstanding stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and have been applied to environmental detection. The ultra-thin layer structure, tiny lateral sizes and abundant surface functional groups make CDs to be ideal capping agents and reducing agents to synthesize metal nanoparticles. CDs with excellent optical properties and electron transfer capabilities have promising applications in the preparation of SERS substrates. Recently, extensive works has been done to construct different SERS substrates using multifunctional CDs as reducing agents [15].

In this work, N-doped carbon dots (DA-CDs) were prepared via a one-pot solvothermal method. DA-CDs as capping agents and reducing agents can catalyze the reaction of HAuCl4 to generate gold nanoparticles (DA-CDs/AuNPs). During this process, the DA-CDs/AuNPs not only have a thinner shell but also grow into colloidal substrates with more uniform size and higher SERS activity by using sodium citrate as second reducing agent (Scheme 1 ). Due to the excellent characteristics of DA-CDs, the morphology of the DA-CDs/AuNPs obtained is flakes. The sharp tips, sharp edges and small cracks of the nanosheets provide large number of “hot spots” for Raman signal enhancement, which makes DA-CDs/AuNPs to exhibit excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of trace amounts of fenamidone in fruits.

Fenamidone, acetamiprid, carbendazim, oxpoconazole, prochloraz, myclobutanil, triadimefon and thiacloprid were bought from the Macklin. Dopamine hydrochloride and hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, ≥99.9 %) were bought from the Aladdin. Methanol, sodium citrate, citric acid, ethylenediamine CaCl2, NaCl, MgCl2, FeCl3, CuCl2, NaNO3, Na2SO4, Na2CO3 and NaHCO3 were purchased from the Fengchuan Chemical reagent Technologies. Apples, pears and grapes were purchased from supermarkets.

The SERS spectra were recorded by an SSR-300 portable Raman instrument equipped with a 785 nm laser source. The UV–vis absorption measurements were carried out using a TU-1901 spectrophotometer in the range 200–800 nm. The fluorescence spectra were collected by an Agilent GS800A fluorescence spectrophotometer. The WX-6000 microwave digestion instrument was used to prepare CDs (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, German).

DA-CDs were prepared by microwave digestion using the following process: Firstly, 25 mg dopamine was accurately weighed into a beaker containing 30 mL water. 2.1 g citric acid and 50 μL ethylenediamine were added with the assistance of ultrasonic wave. Second, the solution was transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave tank. Under 850 W microwave irradiation, the temperature was heated to 180℃ for 60 min. After the reaction was completed, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous phase filter membrane and stored at 4 °C.

DA-CDs/AuNPs were synthesized using a seed-mediated method with modification ([16,17]). Briefly, 200 μL HAuCl4 solution (1 %) and 50 μL of DA-CDs solution (105 mg/mL) were mixed into 30 mL water. The solution was heated to 85 °C and held for 20 min to obtain a stable purple suspension of DA-CDs/AuNPs. DA-CDs/AuNPs were grown by using sodium citrate as a reducing agent. Firstly, 50 μL of sodium citrate solution (0.1 mol/L) was slowly added dropwise to the mixture. Then 200 μL of HAuCl4 (1 %) solution was added dropwise and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. In this process, the color of the DA-CDs/AuNPs was observed to change from purple to brick red. Finally, the resulting DA-CDs/AuNPs colloids were cooled at 4 °C for further experiments.

Based on previous studies [18], fenamidone was sprayed and extracted from the epidermis of the fruit. First, spray various concentrations of fenamidone (0.5, 1 and 2 mg/L) on the surface of the peel by manual atomizer and let it dry naturally. Second, the actual samples (2 g) were crushed and placed in a 15 mL centrifuge tube. Then, 10 mL of acetone was added to the centrifuge tube, sonicated for 30 min, centrifuged for 10 min (8000 rpm). Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm organic filter membrane. The acetone was dried by rotary evaporator in the sample, and the residue was dissolved in 10 mL of methanol solution, thus reducing the interference of the substrate.

First, a methanol stock solution of fenamidone at 1 mg/mL was prepared, and then a number of fenamidone working solutions (20, 15, 10, 5, 1 and 0.5 μg/mL) were diluted with methanol. In practice, an aliquot of 200 μL of DA-CDs/AuNPs was mixed with 100 μL fenamidone solution and the incubation was permitted to proceed for 10 min before the SERS spectra were recorded.

The morphology and dimensions of the synthesized nanomaterial were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1
a, each composite nanoparticle has a low-contrast halo ring associated with a continuous DA-CDs layer wrapping around a high-contrast Au core, revealing the formation of a core–shell structure [19]. However, using DA-CDs as capping agents easily leads to nanomaterial size inhomogeneity and stacking, which may affect the area SERS intensity. It was found that further growth DA-CDs/AuNPs using sodium citrate as a reducing agent, the size of the nanoparticles is more uniform and the stacking is significantly improved (Fig. 1 b). In addition, the thinning of the “shell-core” structure makes it easier for the nanoparticles to bind to the target, which facilitates the generation of “hot spots” and the enhancement of the SERS signal. From the magnified TEM images, nanosheets were dominant in the nanosols (Fig. 1 c), ranging in size from 20 to 45 nm. The sharp tips, sharp edges and small cracks of the nanosheets contribute to considerably large surface areas and provide large number of “hot spots” to strengthen the Raman signal, which makes DA-CDs/AuNPs excellent SERS substrates for fenamidone detection (Fig. 1 d) [20]. The UV–vis spectra of DA-CDs/AuNPs are shown in Fig. 1 e [21]. The color of the DA-CDs/AuNPs colloid changes from violet to burgundy, and the separated spectral band was red-shifted from 536 to 541 nm. The fluorescence spectra of DA-CDs and DA-CDs/AuNPs are displayed in Fig. 1 f. The fluorescence intensity at 448 nm represents the DA-CDs, while DA-CDs/AuNPs shifted to 408 nm. It may be due to charge transfer between DA-CDs and DA-CDs/AuNPs [22]. This indicates the formation of DA-CDs/AuNPs nanomaterials.

The elemental composition and surface functional groups of DA-CDs/AuNPs were explored by XPS spectroscopy (Fig. 1 g). The energy peaks of 284.8 eV, 286 eV and 288.1 eV are caused by C–C, C-O/C-N and C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1 h). The N1s bands of DA-CDs/AuNPs can be separated into two peaks at 399.7 eV and 401.1 eV, which can be attributed to N–H and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. 1 i). The O1s bands consists of a large single peak (Fig. 1 j), the peak at 532 eV is assigned to C-O/C=O, indicating that there are hydroxyl and carboxyl groups around [23–28]. The XPS spectra of Au 4f consists of two peaks at 84.1 eV (Au 4f7/2) and 86.5 eV (Au 4f5/2), indicating that Au3+ was successfully reduced to Au0 (Fig. 1 k). This result further confirms the successful fabrication of the DA-CDs/AuNPs hybrid. The abundance of functional groups on the surface of DA-CDs/AuNPs will better enrichment of fenamidone on the substrate surface through electrostatic interactions or coordination role, which benefit for creating electromagnetic “hot spots” that lead to larger SERS signals [29]. Besides, the Zeta potential of the DA-CDs/AuNPs and DA-CDs/AuNPs + fenamidone were recorded in Fig. 1 l, where the zeta potential values of DA-CDs/AuNPs, DA-CDs/AuNPs + fenamidone were −17 mV and 14 mV, respectively. This indicated that there was a significant interaction between DA-CDs/AuNPs and fenamidone, which led to the enhancement of the SERS signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational procedure and recognition mechanism of fenamidone
ce:section.ce:para:
The Fig. 2
a shows the SERS spectra of DA-CDs/AuNPs for SERS detection of fenamidone at different concentrations. The distinguished peaks of fenamidone at 413, 695, 1186, 1356 and 1578 cm−1 can be clearly noted. The intensity of the SERS signal peaks was found to increase with the concentrations of fenamidone. We recorded SERS spectra of ten samples to explore the repeatability of the DA-CDs/AuNPs substrates in Fig. 2 b. The peak at 1186 cm−1 has a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 5.7 % in Fig. 2 c, indicating good reproducibility and high homogeneity of the DA-CDs/AuNPs as SERS active substrates.

Density general function theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 program to find equilibrium molecular structures and assign Raman modes of the fenamidone molecules. The Lee-Yang-Parr correlation function (LYP) was combined with Becke’s three-parameter exchange function (B3) to perform the calculation. The characteristic peaks of calculated values were compared with the experimental values, and the main wavebands in the spectra are also attributed in Table 1 . The positions of the Raman peak obtained from the fenamidone solution were slightly shifted compared to the simulated Raman spectrum in Fig. 2 d. The signal peak at 698 cm−1 was due to the swing and stretching of C–C on the imidazole ring, while the peak at 410 cm−1 was caused by the non-planar swing of C–C on the benzene ring. The peak at 1186 cm−1 may be attributed to the C–H in-plane rocking. The SERS signal peak at 342 cm−1 was explained by the stretching of the benzene and imidazole rings, while the signal peak at 1354 cm−1 was due to the C-N stretching on the benzene ring. The SERS signal peak at 1416 cm−1 might be ascribed to the N-N stretching on the imidazole ring. The peak at 1576 cm−1 may be ascribed to the N–H rocking.

As shown in Fig. 1 l, the changes of zeta potential reveal that there is a strong interaction between DA-CDs/AuNPs and fenamidone, which could recognize fenamidone. Meanwhile, the “hot spot” caused by DA-CDs is beneficial for the SERS signal of fenamidone by DA-CDs/AuNPs through electromagnetic enhancement mechanism [30]. As shown in Fig. 1 e, the DA-CDs/AuNPs (Growth) shows a higher absorbance than DA-CDs/AuNPs under the same conditions, indicating that the electromagnetic field intensity generated by LSPR will be higher [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fenamidone analytical system
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to achieve better detection results, the reaction conditions of the fenamidone analysis system were evaluated. The effect of DA-CDs/AuNPs amount was investigated according to the procedure. When the concentration of DA-CDs/AuNPs was 0.2 mg/mL, the SERS signal achieved the maximum value (Fig. 3
a-b). Therefore, the DA-CDs/AuNPs amount was selected as 0.2 mg/mL. The effect of reaction time of DA-CDs/AuNPs-fenamidone system was investigated. The SERS signal was strongest when the reaction time was 9 min, so the optimal reaction time was 9 min (Fig. 3 c-d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working curve for SERS detection of fenamidone
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimal conditions, the working curve was plotted according the relationship between fenamidone concentration and Raman intensity values. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the SERS steadily increased with the fenamidone concentration from 0.08 ∼ 2.85 μg/mL. In order to realize quantitative analysis, the obvious Raman peak of fenamidone at 1186 cm−1 was selected to establish a linear relationship. According to Fig. 4 b, the linear regression equation is y = 28099.7746x-950.8534, the correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9925, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.05 μg/mL (LOD is calculated by LOD = 3.3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of blank experiments, and S is the slope of the working curve). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of three replicate experiments was calculated to be 2.88 %. Furthermore, compared with other previously reported methods for imidazole pesticides determination (Table 2 ), reporter probe-based SERS sensor for fenamidone detection exhibited a wider linear range and lower LOD. Therefore, this method can meet the routine detection and quantification requirements of fenamidone residues in most samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and specificity of the DA-CDs/AuNPs-based SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the selectivity and interference immunity of the sensor, fenamidone and structural analogues (acetamiprid, carbendazim, oxpoconazole, prochloraz, myclobutanil, triadimefon and thiacloprid) were further researched in the present work (Fig. 5
a). The results indicated that the structurally similar pesticides did not show significant SERS signals. The potential interference for fenamidone assay was investigated using coexisting substances in the fruit, including sugar and other carbohydrates. These results showed that the coexisting substances in the fruit did not produce considerable interference peaks in the SERS spectra (Fig. 5 b). In addition, the effect of ions, including Ca2+ Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, NO3 –, SO4 2- CO3 2– and HCO3 –, was investigated (Fig. 5 c-d). The result showed that most ions interfere less with the SERS determination of fenamidone, except CO3 2– and HCO3 –. When Ca2+ and NaOH + Ca2+ are added to the reaction system, the interferences of CO3 2– and HCO3 – should be eliminated. These results indicated that the SERS analysis method based on DA-CDs/AuNPs had excellent selectivity due to the specific binding to fenamidone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of fenamidone on various fruit peels
ce:section.ce:para:
The possibility of using the proposed SERS sensor for the detection of fenamidone in various fruit samples was investigated. The SERS mapping of the fruit samples is shown in Fig. 6
a. It can be noticed that the Raman spectra of the blank samples showed no significant signal, but the Raman signal intensity of the spiked samples was lower compared to the standard solution (Fig. 6 b-d). It is possible that part of the DA-CDs/AuNPs matrix interacts with carbohydrates, which interferes with the measurement of pesticide molecules. However, the main Raman peaks of fenamidone have no obvious change. The overall recovery values were between 90.6 % and 98.7 % (Table 3 ), which exhibited the high accuracy of the method. Glucose, fructose and natural colors in apples, pears and grapes did not cause interrupt in the DA-CDs/AuNPs based SERS detection. The measured values were consistent with the theoretical values on the fruit, demonstrating the reliability of this method.

In summary, a novel core–shell structured SERS substrate was synthesized by using N-doped dopamine CDs (DA-CDs) as a reducing and stabilizing agent. The grown DA-CDs/AuNPs nanosheets not only have thinner shells, but are also more uniform in size. The surface of DA-CDs/AuNPs was rich in oxygen-containing groups, sharp tips, as well as sharp edges and small cracks, which contribute to a considerable surface area and provide a large number of “hot spots”, thus exhibiting high SERS activity. Coupled with the specific combination of fenamidone and DA-CDs/AuNPs, a convenient analysis method was created. The method has the benefits of simple operation, high flexibility, and strong anti-interference ability, and has been successfully used for the detection of fenamidone in actual samples.

Kexiang Li: Writing – original draft. Hong Li: Writing – original draft, Software. Qian Zhang: Supervision. Dezhi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Yaling Yang: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117) and the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2021T20200097).
